data.title: 61 million passenger trips at Hong Kong airport in 2025, up 15% year on year
data.description:
Passenger trips at the Hong Kong International Airport reached 61 million in 2025, marking a 15 per cent year-on-year increase, after a full year of operation with the facility’s third runway.
The Airport Authority said on Thursday that the strong figures came on the back of a busy Christmas season, which saw over 200,000 daily passenger trips over eight days in December. Cargo throughput for the year also rose by 2.7 per cent to 5.07 million tonnes.
“The strong traffic performance in 2025 marks a new milestone, securing our position as the leading international aviation hub in Asia,” the authority’s CEO Vivian Cheung Kar-fay said, adding that the authority had won 80 awards last year.
“During the year, we also further strengthened connectivity by introducing 30 new destinations across the globe, including Abu Dhabi, Brussels and Dallas, while increasing flight frequencies on major routes,” she said.
Flight movements in 2025 rose by 8.7 per cent compared with the previous year, reaching 394,730, with the authority highlighting how the launch of the Terminal 2 coach hall and the “Park and Fly” service facilitated smoother transport for passengers in the Greater Bay Area.
According to the authority, in December alone, the airport handled 5.8 million passengers, a 13.5 per cent increase compared with the same period last year, while flight movements rose 7.1 per cent year on year to 35,920.
The rise in passenger volume came after the airport’s third runway system was launched in November 2024.
The authority said the number of passengers transiting through Hong Kong also recorded double-digit growth in December. While it did not reveal the actual figure, the authority noted that traffic to and from mainland China, Southeast Asia and North America recorded the biggest jumps.
The airport’s cargo volume also grew 3.6 per cent year on year to 462,000 tonnes in December. Exports and transshipments both increased during the month, with transshipments recording 18 per cent growth.
Cargo traffic to and from Southeast Asia, Europe and the mainland increased the most among key trading regions.

With the HK$141.5 billion (US$18.1 billion) third runway project, the airport’s passenger throughput capacity is expected to be raised in phases by 50 per cent to 120 million annually by around 2035, while the cargo volume will be doubled from its current 5 million tonnes to 10 million tonnes per year.
The boosted capacity is set to enhance the airport’s competitiveness in the region, particularly in the Greater Bay Area, Beijing’s plan to combine Hong Kong, Macau and nine cities in Guangdong province into a new economic engine.
Former lawmaker Gary Zhang Xinyu, a member of the Legislative Council’s transport affairs panel, told the Post previously that the term “hub” referred not just to Hong Kong as a point of arrival or departure, but to travellers’ preference for transiting through the city because of the airport’s efficiency.

data.title: Pakistan’s arms deals cleared for take-off as JF-17 orders soar from Muslim countries
data.description:
Pakistan is increasingly confident of making billion-dollar arms sales, headlined by JF-17 fighters co-produced with China, as up to six Muslim-majority countries look to upgrade their air forces amid growing geopolitical turbulence and civil wars.
A preliminary deal worth US$4 billion for an unspecified number of JF-17s and other training aircraft manufactured by Pakistan Aeronautical Complex was struck last month with the Libyan National Army, according to Reuters reports citing Aamir Masood, an unofficial spokesman for Pakistan’s military.
The regime, backed by Egypt, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates, controls Libya’s east. It rivals the internationally recognised Islamist government based in the capital Tripoli, which is supported by Qatar and Turkey.
Masood, a retired Pakistan Air Force air marshal, has also described a prospective US$1.5 billion sale of light attack aircraft, surveillance and suicide drones – and possibly JF-17s – to Sudan’s military government as a “done deal”. This would boost Khartoum’s prospects of gaining a decisive edge over the rebel Rapid Support Forces.
Pakistan is also discussing a US$4 billion arms deal with Saudi Arabia, according to Masood. Both countries signed a mutual defence pact in September, soon after Israeli warplanes bombed Hamas negotiators in Qatar.
Islamabad has reportedly proposed a US$2 billion arms-for-debt component, which might include Riyadh financing the Sudan deal. The proposal is politically complicated because, while Saudi Arabia, Turkey and Egypt support the military regime, the UAE backs the Rapid Support Forces.

In recent weeks, Pakistan’s military leadership has discussed selling JF-17s to their counterparts from Bangladesh, Indonesia and Iraq.
The fighter jets were credited with destroying an advanced Russian S-400 air defence system during Pakistan’s brief air war with India in May.
Shortly after the conflict, Azerbaijan expanded its 2024 order for JF-17s from 16 to 40 aircraft in a US$4.6 billion deal spanning training, technical support and weaponry – by far the biggest ever order for the planes, as well as for Pakistan defence equipment. It took delivery of five of the aircraft in December.
Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif’s administration hopes the sales, if confirmed, will alleviate Pakistan’s long-standing problem of earning foreign exchange and reduce its perpetual need for loans from the International Monetary Fund (IMF), strategic ally China and mutual defence pact partner Saudi Arabia, among others.
“There are several countries that are actively engaged in talks with Pakistan on this, and this will boost our defence production and benefit our economy,” Sharif told his cabinet at a meeting in Islamabad on Wednesday, according to an official handout.
Cost-effective fighter
Pakistan’s defence deals “can be understood along at least three distinct vectors”, Fahd Humayun, an assistant professor of political science at Tufts University, told This Week in Asia.
For Pakistan’s leaders, arms agreements with countries like Saudi Arabia, Libya and Sudan “in the aggregate are likely influenced by long-term economic considerations”.
Pakistan is currently under a US$7 billion IMF bailout programme agreed in 2024, the country’s 28th since 1950, after it narrowly avoided a Sri Lanka-like default on international payments with help from China, Saudi Arabia, the UAE and Qatar.
Whether defence exports translate into reduced external dependence, though, “remains to be seen”, according to Humayun. It will depend on Pakistan’s indigenous and joint production capacities, platform delivery timelines and, most importantly, the nature of the deals that are eventually signed.
Pakistani leaders are also hoping to accrue geopolitical goodwill, with the reported agreements signalling “expanded strategic influence in the Muslim world, reflecting an effort by Pakistan to position itself as a reliable supplier to states facing political, financial or logistical constraints in accessing Western systems”, he said.


Finally, the interest of so many countries in the JF-17 is a “testament” to the Pakistani air force’s performance against India in May, Humayun said.
The lightweight fighter provided near-4.5 generation capabilities “in the most cost-effective platform available in the world right now”, he added.
Pakistan’s defence production minister Raza Hayat Harraj told BBC Urdu on Wednesday that the JF-17 was priced at between US$40 million and US$50 million.
According to military experts, that is about half the price of full-fledged 4.5 generation warplanes manufactured by the West, although their planes – like France’s Rafale and the Eurofighter Typhoon – are bigger, have better avionics and engines, and have greater range and can carry bigger payloads.
This places the Pakistani fighters in the same category as the Chinese J-10Cs that, operating in a datalinked formation with JF-17s, shot down several Indian planes, including one Rafale, during a skirmish involving some 110 aircraft in May.
Risks and rewards
“The lining up of potential buyers in quick succession is a vote of confidence in Pakistani air defence capabilities, in a difficult neighbourhood where the risk of another conflict breaking out remains high,” Humayun said.
But it would likely take several years for Pakistan’s prospective sales to mature into confirmed orders and deliveries to commence, Muhammad Faisal, a South Asia security researcher based at the University of Technology Sydney, told This Week in Asia.
A case in point is Malaysia, where despite JF-17 participation in Malaysian Air Force trials in 2018-19, a sales agreement was not reached.

Selling to clients involved in civil wars and backed by rival Middle Eastern governments was also fraught with risks.
Humayun said deals with Libyan and Sudanese factions operating under international sanctions could “expose Pakistan to legal, reputational and geopolitical costs”.
“The risk for Pakistan concurrently is of being pulled, directly or indirectly, into proxy dynamics it does not control,” he said.
Pakistan would “have to be careful and coordinate closely with its Gulf partners” when navigating the Libyan and Sudanese conflict space, Faisal said.
However, such risks remained in any conflict space and arms deals often were worth the reward for the seller, he added.
“More than risks and rewards, Pakistan is now implementing a smart Middle East policy in close coordination with Saudi Arabia and Turkey as the primary partners.”

data.title: Japan, Philippines tighten ties with new defence pact amid China tensions
data.description:
Japan and the Philippines signed a defence pact on Thursday that would allow the tax-free provision of ammunition, fuel, food and other necessities when their forces stage joint training to boost deterrence against China’s growing aggression in the region and to bolster their preparation for natural disasters.
Japan has faced increasing political, trade and security tensions with Beijing, which was angered by Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remark that potential mainland Chinese action against Taiwan could spark Japanese intervention.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. While many nations, including the US, do not officially acknowledge Taiwan as an independent state, they oppose any use of force to alter the existing status quo.
Japan and the Philippines have also had separate territorial conflicts with Beijing in the East China Sea and South China Sea that have continued to flare and threaten to draw in the United States, a treaty ally of the two Asian nations.
Japanese Foreign Minister Toshimitsu Motegi signed the Acquisition and Cross-Servicing Agreement with Philippine Foreign Secretary Theresa Lazaro in Manila. During the ceremony, Japan also announced new security and economic development help to the Philippines.
The military logistical agreement, which has to be ratified by Japanese legislators before it takes effect, is the latest key defence pact to be forged between Japan and the Philippines to deepen their security alliance.
Aside from easing joint combat drills, it would also help Japan and the Philippines jointly respond to natural disasters, a mutual concern, and participate in peacekeeping operations by the United Nations, according to Japanese and Philippine officials.


In mid-2024, both countries signed the Reciprocal Access Agreement, which allows the deployment of forces of either country to the other’s territory for joint and larger combat exercises, including live-fire drills. The RAA took effect in September.
Japanese and Philippine officials are still negotiating another agreement that aims to boost the security of highly confidential defence and military information the countries could share.
When Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr and then Japanese prime minister Shigeru Ishiba jointly announced the start of negotiations for the Acquisition and Cross-servicing Agreement in Manila in April last year, Ishiba underscored the opposition by their countries of “any attempt to unilaterally change the status quo through force or coercion in the East and South China seas”.
Ishiba’s statement then was an obvious reprimand of China, though he did not name the country. “I hope that our two countries will continue to work closely together to realise a free and open Indo-Pacific based on the rule of law,” Ishiba said.
Chinese and Philippine coastguard ships have had increasingly hostile confrontations in the South China Sea under Marcos, who took office in 2022. His predecessor, Rodrigo Duterte, nurtured cosy ties with Chinese President Xi Jinping and Russian leader Vladimir Putin.


Beijing claims virtually the entire waterway, where it has bolstered its coastguard and naval presence and built artificial island bases to fortify its claims. Other countries including Malaysia and Vietnam have also been involved in the long-seething territorial stand-offs.
In the East China Sea, China has routinely sent coastguard vessels and planes into waters and airspace that surround islands, which are claimed by both Tokyo and Beijing, to harass Japanese vessels. That has prompted Japan at times to scramble jets in response.
The US has repeatedly warned China over its escalating acts of aggression in the disputed waters against Japan and the Philippines, which are among Washington’s staunchest treaty allies in Asia.

data.title: Hong Kong consumer watchdog urges better elderly services after 9,206 complaints
data.description:
Hong Kong’s consumer watchdog has called on businesses to improve services for elderly customers after receiving more than 9,200 complaints in the past three years, including a woman who spent HK$400,000 (US$51,294) on beauty treatments.
The Consumer Council said on Thursday that improving services for seniors would help boost the spending potential of the “silver economy”, a priority for the city’s government amid a rapidly ageing population.
“The council urges traders to review their sales policies and provide tailored support for senior consumers,” it said.
“At the same time, the council reminds senior consumers to consider their actual needs when enticed with sales promotions, avoid purchasing excessive services or products, read and understand the relevant sales terms carefully before the transaction, and not rush into paying.”
Between 2023 and 2025, the watchdog received 9,206 complaints from seniors, defined as those aged 55 or above, with 2024 seeing the highest number at 3,827.
In one case, a woman in her 70s told the council that she spent more than HK$300,000 on beauty treatments between 2022 and 2023. But despite unfinished sessions, beauty parlour staff members continued to press her to purchase more, without mentioning remaining services or expiry dates.
Over the next two years, she bought an additional HK$105,000 worth of services and had made a partial payment of HK$79,000.
When she asked about unused treatments, she discovered she had more than 550 sessions left, due to expire in two years. The watchdog noted that even if she visited the beauty parlour every two weeks, it would take more than 20 years to use them all.

When approached by the council, the beauty parlour said it had clearly explained the non-refundable terms. It agreed to cancel the HK$26,000 outstanding balance, but the elderly complainant refused and was advised to seek legal advice.
In another case, an 80-year-old man spent HK$12,500 on a Smart TV based on a sales representative’s recommendation, despite little experience with such devices. The salesman assured the elderly that an installation technician would show him how to use it.
But on installation day, the technician reportedly left immediately without explaining how to use the device. When the man tried the new television, he received an error message.
When he called the shop for help, he was told a technician could come to inspect the device within two days. But after a week, he still had not received any follow-up.
When contacted by the council, the television brand’s agent agreed to provide a free in-home tutorial for the complainant.
The watchdog advised seniors to tell staff about their familiarity with smart devices when buying such products and to request a demonstration during installation.
It also reminded them to base beauty service purchases on actual needs and avoid hasty decisions. Seniors are encouraged to have family members review their bills and accompany them when signing contracts.
For businesses, the council said sellers of smart devices should consider enhancing technical support for seniors by providing clear demonstrations and usage guidance during installation.
It added that sellers of treatments or other contractual services should explain complex terms clearly and use a larger font to help seniors read more easily.
“Be compassionate and only recommend products and services for senior consumers which are suitable for their needs and abilities. Refrain from aggressively selling products which they may not be able to use,” it said.

data.title: Paul Lafayet co-founder’s new Hong Kong bakery Social Goods combines his biggest passions
data.description:
In 2009, Christophe Younes left Paris for Hong Kong at the age of 17 to finish high school. Fresh-faced and eager, and having never lived abroad, he was going to join his father in the city.
He had not even moved on to university when his father first suggested opening a patisserie. That planted a seed that would see the family opening the first Paul Lafayet in 2016, transforming patisserie in Hong Kong from a rarefied luxury mostly confined to hotel lobbies into an accessible street-level pleasure for all.
Now, after opening 12 branches in the city and three more in Shanghai, Younes is boldly striking out with a new concept.
Social Goods, a bakery and cafe in Central on Hong Kong Island, represents his biggest life passions: it is a space where the ethos of the gym meets the warmth of a kitchen that smells like home.
“For me, food has always been two things: fuel and comfort,” he says. “My life is split between the discipline of fitness and the joy of cooking. This place is where they finally meet.”

Growing up in a culinary household, he was always surrounded by a deep love of cooking from an early age.
“It’s always been in the family. My mom, my grandma, everyone cooks and loves food. My father decided to open a French patisserie [in Hong Kong] because he missed having it and there weren’t a lot of them here back then.”
Younes has also always been interested in sports. In 2014, after his friends’ suggestion, he set his sights on completing the gruelling Oxfam Trailwaker race along the MacLehose Trail, the longest in Hong Kong at 100km (62 miles). After training for a year only to miss the race with a knee injury, his resolve did not falter – it intensified. He committed to running every long-distance race he could find, going on to conquer the 100km trail in 2015, finishing in just over 24 hours.
Soon, Younes sought a new frontier, and he found it in Olympic weightlifting – an explosive, technical sport featuring barbell movements such as the snatch and the clean and jerk. His entry into the sport was as spontaneous as his start in trail running.
“I was going to the gym, just lifting weights, right? I’ve always liked it, but I never made a sport out of it. And then there was this really cute girl,” he laughs. “She was doing [Olympic weightlifting] and I thought I could use that as an excuse to talk to her.
“She helped me train for a bit before she left for the US, but I kept going. So instead of getting the girl, I got the sport!”

Six years later, Younes has become a competitive force, regularly ranking among the strongest lifters in Hong Kong. In the 2023 Hong Kong Weightlifting Open, he placed second overall in the Sinclair Rankings, a system used in Olympic weightlifting to compare the relative strength of athletes across all body weight categories.
“For me, it’s always been a discipline, and not just aesthetics,” he says.
This approach extends to his lifestyle. He maintains a strict schedule, going to bed by 10pm and waking at 6am – a rhythm that aligns perfectly with the predawn world of a baker.
Perhaps the most revolutionary element of Younes’ fitness journey is his nutritional philosophy, which shatters the stereotype of the restrictive athlete. A certified nutrition coach, he champions a principle of “smart indulgence”.
This philosophy is the bedrock of Social Goods, where he reimagines indulgence through a lens of quality and balance. The menu reflects his personal rules: no sugary drinks, no deep-fried items, and pastries crafted to be “as pure and light as possible”, with minimal sugar without sacrificing flavour.
“There’s no way I’m giving up sweets,” he states unequivocally. “The idea was, how can I eat as much as I want in the smartest way possible?
“I have people asking me all the time how I can eat so much bread and so many pastries while still staying in shape. It’s simple: you just have to know what you’re eating.”

The bakery offers a wide variety of baked goods, ranging from classics like pain au chocolat and Basque cheesecake to the more experimental “eggies” – what Younes describes as egg tarts baked in the shape of whole eggs.
There is also a build-your-own brunch plate option, structured like Younes’ own balanced meal plan: high on protein and fibre while, of course, absolutely not skimping on carbohydrates.
The real star of the show, however, is the sourdough.
Moving beyond mere tradition, Younes has created what he terms “version three” of bread.
He breaks it down like this: commercial white bread is version one, sourdough is version two. His offering is the next step: sourdough made exclusively with a curated selection of ancient grains such as spelt and einkorn.
“They were used a long time ago and were kind of put out of the market because they were harder to harvest,” he explains.

The result is six different flavours of sourdough offered at Social Goods: cheddar jalapeño, chorizo and olives, turmeric and pumpkin seeds, pistachio fig, matcha, and Earl Grey.
The choice is driven by a dual focus on digestibility and gut health, a priority born from his fitness journey and background in nutritional studies. Younes believes the superior fibre content and different gluten structure of these grains make the bread fundamentally better for the body. “Using healthier flour means healthier digestion,” he says.
He notes that nowadays the trend in Hong Kong is for people to eat healthily. “Only 10 years ago, sourdough wasn’t so common in Hong Kong, but I think now people love it.”
Helping to guide his vision is his 68-year-old mother, a former nurse who always dreamed of working in a bakery and now works alongside him. Coincidentally, sourdough is her speciality.
“She’s the real master. She knows quite a bit, and she’ll even teach the rookies sometimes.”
Also a member of the team is his older brother, Merwann, a designer who brought the vision to life – from the digital prints on the tiles and the vinyl records on display to the integration of the preserved pillars of the 60-year-old building itself. Younes says that he “couldn’t have done it without him”.

Together, built on the foundation of Paul Lafayet, Social Goods is another Younes family affair but one that introduces a new concept: the importance of community.
That is reflected in both the enterprise’s name and attitude. Laptops are banned to foster conversation. Future plans include yoga collaborations, thrift-shop Sundays and pet adoption days – initiatives meant to build a community, not just a customer base.
“This is everything I am,” Christophe Younes says. “It’s the family table, powered by good people.”
Social Goods, L/G, Sun Lee Building, 43 Wellington St (entrance on Pottinger St), Central.

data.title: Chinese military says it is developing over 10 quantum warfare weapons
data.description:
China’s military says it is using quantum technology to gather high-value military intelligence from public cyberspace.
The People’s Liberation Army said more than 10 experimental quantum cyber warfare tools were “under development”, many of which were being “tested in front-line missions”, according to the official newspaper Science and Technology Daily.
The project is being led by a supercomputing laboratory at the National University of Defence Technology, according to the report, with a focus on cloud computing, artificial intelligence and quantum technology.
Commanders hope that quantum computing will allow them to process large amounts of battlefield data within seconds, helping them to make decisions and allocate resources.
Quantum sensing and positioning technologies could also improve air defences by detecting stealth aircraft that conventional radar systems may miss.
The technology could also be used to create highly accurate navigation systems that are resistant to spoofing or jamming.
An unnamed officer told the newspaper that “speed and change” were the main considerations in developing new cyber weapons.
The officer added that the principle behind the development of these weapons was that “to design a good weapon, one must first think about the form of the next war”.
Liu Wei, a researcher at the PLA’s Information Support Force, told the newspaper the research team was working on “new models of battlefield awareness powered by cybersecurity”.
The newspaper said: “[The team] fully leverages the advantages of the public service platform system’s integration and all-domain support capabilities”.
The report said researchers from the unit had been working along with front-line troops to better understand their needs and had used individual pieces of data collected over the course of a year to produce a unified situation map.
“In the future, we will continue to maintain a close partnership with the military to better serve them,” Liu said.
The report also quoted a senior figure in a quantum computing and supercomputing unit, who said “we must enhance our awareness of operational needs and maintain sensitivity in the use of technology”.
The article added: “Since its establishment, the unit has actively adapted to changes in warfare, technology and adversaries, and addressed combat needs with technology-driven applications.”
It also said the team had “promoted the dynamic tracking, results sorting and practical application of cutting-edge technologies in the field of cybersecurity and information technology”.

data.title: Japan should put more eggs in the renewable basket
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
Japan’s restarting of the world’s biggest nuclear power plant is a bold response to energy insecurity, economic stagnation and climate imperatives. In contrast to intermittent, weather-dependent renewable sources such as wind and solar, nuclear power offers reliable 24/7 electricity – crucial to bolstering Tokyo’s industrial competitiveness.
In the face of structural constraints on large-scale renewable deployment, the restart could reduce Japan’s reliance on imported coal and liquefied natural gas, which cost about US$67 billion last year, while helping Tokyo to mitigate geopolitical risks by limiting exposure to external shocks and supply chain disruptions.
Yet nuclear power alone cannot secure Japan’s energy future. Renewables must expand faster. Removing approval delays, overcoming land-use challenges and reducing market uncertainties are essential in scaling solar, wind and other proven technologies.
The country’s strategic energy plan for 2040 – with an energy mix including renewables at up to 50 per cent and nuclear at 20 per cent – offers policy stability to businesses and promotes decarbonisation.
Tokyo’s focus on next-generation technologies like perovskite solar cells and nuclear fusion reflects a desire to reduce dependence on Chinese-made solar panels. China dominates the manufacturing and supply chains of solar panels and wind turbines and is rapidly becoming a nuclear powerhouse, with substantial investments in fusion.
But perovskite and fusion remain years from commercial viability. With the former, durability and cost remain issues. The latter may take decades to reach the grid.
Nuclear energy can deliver high-capacity, low-carbon baseload power but the financial challenges cannot be ignored. Taking into consideration the construction and lifetime operating costs of nuclear plants, the levelised cost of electricity for advanced nuclear power was estimated at $110/MWh in 2023 and is expected to remain unchanged up to 2050. Even nuclear-heavy France plans for renewables to provide 33 per cent of its energy consumption by 2030.
While next-generation technologies could lay the foundation for Japan’s long-term resilience, the country shouldn’t lag behind in a critical sector like renewables in the near term.
Azhar Azam, Lahore, Pakistan
Let’s do better on paper and plastic bottle recycling
The waste statistics for 2024, released last month, show both encouraging progress and pressing challenges in Hong Kong’s waste management. The per capita disposal rate fell to 1.4kg per day, the second lowest level in a decade, yet several trends require close scrutiny.
Foremost among these is a surge in paper waste. While the phasing out of single-use plastics led to plastic tableware waste falling by a notable 14.8 per cent to 207 tonnes per day, many businesses appear to have substituted plastics with paper-based alternatives. Consequently, paper waste rose by about 12 per cent to 2,431 tonnes per day and is the second largest category of landfill waste.
The forthcoming EcoPark recycling plant is expected to process much of this locally. However, reports indicate that the suppliers’ minimum price of HK$1,200 per tonne is insufficient to sustain the entire used paper supply chain. The authorities must stabilise the frontline recyclers’ price to defend both environmental objectives and the livelihoods of grass-roots workers, including scavengers, and ensure the plant represents a genuine step forward.
Food waste trends are more positive. The daily amount of food disposed in 2024 was 3,001 tonnes, 6 per cent down from 2023. By the end of 2024, about 1,200 smart recycling bins had been installed, collecting around 75,040 tonnes of food waste – nearly three-quarters of the total recycled. Overall, 104,800 tonnes of food waste were recycled, equivalent to half of Hong Kong’s treatment capacity of 219,000 tonnes annually.
While public participation is strong, further policy measures are required to maximise the efficiency of existing infrastructure.
Plastic bottles remain a significant concern. The amount disposed rose by 9 per cent to 217 tonnes per day, despite the reverse vending machine pilot scheme.
In 2024, Hong Kong used an estimated 1.6 billion plastic bottles, yet only 64.5 million bottles were collected through 120 machines, representing a recycling rate of 4 per cent.
The government is suggesting a rebate of at least 10 cents and aiming for a 30 per cent recycling rate for plastic containers and 10 per cent for cartons.
Singapore’s Beverage Container Return Scheme, to be launched this year with 1,000 return points and refunds of 10 S cents (60 HK cents), targets a return rate of 80 per cent by 2029.
Hong Kong should adopt similarly ambitious measures, with refunds between 50 HK cents and HK$1, to align with the goals of the Waste Blueprint for Hong Kong 2035.
Rico Wong, executive director, The Green Earth

data.title: All Blacks fire coach Robertson less than two years before World Cup after scathing review
data.description:
New Zealand fired coach Scott Robertson on Thursday less than two years before the Rugby World Cup, after a scathing 2025 performance review and rumours of a player revolt.
The 51-year-old led the team to 20 wins in 27 matches, below powerhouse New Zealand’s expectations, while showing little improvement during his two years in charge.
New Zealand Rugby’s internal review of performance found strong criticism from senior All Blacks, including a suggestion that star player Ardie Savea could walk out.
New Zealand Rugby chair David Kirk said it was the right time to make a change, with the 2027 Rugby World Cup the “key goal”.
“The midpoint in the Rugby World Cup cycle is the right time to look at the All Blacks’ progress over the first two seasons,” Kirk said.
“We’ve taken an extensive look at the team’s progress on and off the field and have subsequently had discussions with Scott on the way forward.

“Both NZR and Scott agree it is in the best interests of the team that he depart his role as head coach.
“On behalf of New Zealand Rugby, I would like to thank Scott for his contribution to the All Blacks.
“As always, he has continued to put the All Blacks first and we respect that he has done the hard but right thing in agreeing to depart.”
Robertson said in a statement he was “gutted” to be leaving the team, but he was “incredibly proud” of what they had achieved.
“Coaching the All Blacks has been the honour of my life,” he said.
“As you can imagine, I am gutted by this outcome. I care deeply about this team.”
Robertson said he had ushered through a talented group of young players, improving depth and setting good foundations for the future.
But he said he agreed to leave after reflecting on the end-of-year review.
Robertson took over as coach in January 2024 with a contract through to the end of the 2027 World Cup in Australia.
His appointment was greeted with much fanfare after he led the Canterbury Crusaders to seven consecutive Super Rugby titles with a reputation as a forward-thinking leader who had strong support from his players.
He endeared himself to fans with his winning record and his habit of breakdancing on the pitch after his team’s title wins.
But under his leadership, the All Blacks have been in a spin.

Robertson lost two of his assistant coaches in his first two years, with Leon MacDonald and Jason Holland quitting, citing differences with the head coach.
Robertson claimed last year that his authority had been undermined, saying that his assistant, Scott Hansen, “effectively operates in the role most would recognise as the head coach”.
But Chris Lendrum, New Zealand Rugby’s professional rugby and performance general manager, said the comments were taken “out of context”.
Otago Highlanders coach Jamie Joseph, the former Japan coach, or former Australia coach Dave Rennie could be potential successors to oversee a packed Test calendar in 2026, in the run-up to the World Cup.
The All Blacks travel to South Africa for an eight-match tour in August and September, playing the Springboks four times.
They also host France, Italy and Ireland in the Nations Cup in July, then play Australia home and away in the Bledisloe Cup before travelling to Europe in November for more Nations Cup matches.

data.title: China’s C919 airliner put through paces by test pilots from European aviation regulator
data.description:
European aviation evaluators have begun test flights of China’s home-grown C919 airliner in Shanghai, the Post has learned, as the manufacturer of the narrowbody jet presses ahead with its bid to secure Western safety certification – a crucial step in the journey to global adoption and being able to compete with industry leaders Boeing and Airbus.
Two test pilots from the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) have conducted verification flights, according to a source with knowledge of the arrangements. Meanwhile, senior foreign pilots working in China have been deployed to help demonstrate the C919’s reliability.
The moves, following a delay in the high-stakes certification process last year, were part of ongoing exchanges between Commercial Aircraft Corporation of China (Comac) – the C919’s manufacturer – and the European regulator, the source said.
EASA pilots conducted in-flight tests in November on a C919 that took off from Shanghai’s Pudong Airport, near the aircraft’s production base, the source added.
“[EASA] agreed the aircraft is good and safe … other than some teething problems that required tweaks,” the source said.
Another source confirmed the test flights and said there had been an increase in exchanges between Comac and EASA recently.
Comac and EASA have not responded to requests for comment from the Post.
Jason Li Hanming, an aviation and logistics analyst, said progress was being made and EASA’s test flights would be crucial.
“They will focus on how the aircraft can handle extreme situations for real-time, airborne assessment,” he said. “It is arguably the most visible part of the certification, signalling the efforts by both Comac and EASA.”
Flight tests are a key part of compliance demonstration, the third stage of EASA’s four-part assessment regime. They validate critical aspects as the aircraft is put through extreme manoeuvres, stalls and weather environments. After successful flight testing and other demonstrations, EASA reviews the complete compliance package and ensures any issues are resolved before issuing certification.


The C919, China’s challenger to the Boeing 737 and Airbus A320 mainline narrowbody airliners, still needs Western certification to bolster its international standing and fly beyond Chinese skies. That is despite more than two years of smooth domestic operations since May 2023, with four million passengers transported by the end of last year.
In May last year, EASA executive director Florian Guillermet said the approval timeline for the C919 could be extended to “three to six years”, dashing hopes the Chinese airliner would pass inspection soon.
“The C919 cannot be certified in 2025 … We should be certifying within three to six years,” Guillermet said, while adding he was optimistic the plane would eventually receive certification.
“Comac is putting a lot of resources, determination and technical means into this certification,” he said. “I have no doubt they will succeed.”
In recent months, Comac has also tapped Western pilots employed by Chinese airlines to operate C919 flight simulators and participate in technical dialogue with EASA.
“It’s just started as the first check for the CRM (crew resource management) assessment,” the first source said. “The first check was passed, with EASA satisfied about the results, but there will be more.”
CRM refers to a set of procedures, principles and skills for the use of all available resources – crew members, hardware and information – to ensure safety and reduce human error. EASA mandates CRM as part of evaluations of human factor considerations and cockpit design for aircraft type certification.
The first source said that some software issues related to interactions between the crew and the aircraft had been identified in recent inspections, but rectification would only require “small fixes” and no hardware problems were involved.
Li said the mobilisation of Western pilots was part of the manufacturer’s efforts to recruit a range of aviation professionals to make a stronger case for the C919.
“Those foreign captains are elite, with a wealth of experience, and their participation can enhance Comac’s exchanges with EASA and offer valuable input,” he said.

data.title: Unexpected Family movie review: Jackie Chan trades kicks for tears in sentimental drama
data.description:
3/5 stars
Jackie Chan strives to paint himself as a respectable father figure yet again in his new comedy drama Unexpected Family, playing an elderly man struggling with Alzheimer’s disease who mistakes a young loner for his own estranged son.
Short on action but long on ham-fisted life lessons, the debut feature from writer-director Li Taiyan – credited simply as “Tai” – seems tailor-made for the Chinese holiday season, with its fast-paced blend of slapstick humour, domestic squabbles and climactic melodrama.
Ren Jiqing (Chan) is a former athlete with fading faculties who lives a quiet life in Wuhan, China. He is surrounded by a motley group of sympathetic neighbours, including Aunt Jin (Li Ping), Xiaoyue (Karlina Zhang Jianing) and reformed gangster-turned-mechanic Little Jia (Pan Binlong).


When scruffy out-of-towner Bufan (Peng Yuchang) appears on Jia’s doorstep looking for a job, he is offered lodgings in Ren’s storeroom, only for the confused senior to mistake him for his own son, Zhuangzhuang.
Zhuangzhuang was a competitive weightlifter who, 30 years earlier, failed to secure a world championship. It is an episode that still haunts Ren’s fragmented memory.
Overjoyed that his “son” has apparently returned after more than a decade away, Ren takes Bufan in. When Bufan discovers there is an inheritance waiting for him, the neighbours agree to play along with the ruse.
In the film’s first half, Ren’s condition is treated as little more than bumbling forgetfulness and used as a catalyst for a string of comedic high jinks.

Later on, Tai and his cast appear to have been reminded that Alzheimer’s is no laughing matter, and as Ren’s health worsens, the film takes a dramatic – if not unforeseen – turn towards the tragic.
To his credit, Chan is surprisingly convincing as Ren – despite the script offering little understanding of how to actually care for someone with Alzheimer’s and the actor being hampered by some distracting de-ageing make-up during numerous flashbacks.
The seasoned action star steps up admirably, bringing genuine emotion to a climax that forces the father to reconsider how he handled the public disgrace of his famous son.
The themes in Unexpected Family might be delivered with all the subtlety of a 200lb barbell, but they are well intentioned and hard to contest. Families need not be bonded by blood, but are indispensable support structures to be nurtured rather than exploited.

If Chan’s cinematic output over the last decade is any indication, he is eager to demonstrate that he has finally got the message. Based on his performance in Unexpected Family, this reviewer is inclined to agree with him.
Want more articles like this? Follow SCMP Film on Facebook
